title: Carpal tunnel
body_html:
Definition
Carpal Tunnel Syndrome (CTS) is the most common entrapment neuropathy, caused by compression of the median nerve as it passes through the carpal tunnel at the wrist, a fibro-osseous canal formed by the carpal bones on the floor and sides and the transverse carpal ligament (flexor retinaculum) on the roof. This fibro-osseous canal contains the median nerve and flexor tendons. Compression leads to sensory and motor deficits in the median nerve distribution.

Contents of the carpal tunnel:
  • Median Nerve
  • Flexor Digitorum Superficialis (4 tendons)
  • Flexor Digitorum Profundus (4 tendons)
  • Flexor Pollicis Longus

Illustration of carpal tunnel. Click on image to enlarge

Pathophysiology
  • Increased pressure in the carpal tunnel (normal ~2–10 mmHg) leads to median nerve ischaemia and demyelination.
  • Chronic compression can cause axonal loss and muscle wasting.
  • Risk factors increase intracarpal pressure or reduce canal size, including repetitive wrist flexion/extension, fluid retention, or structural anomalies.

Risk Factors
  • Female sex (middle age)
  • Repetitive hand/wrist activity (typing, manual labor)
  • Pregnancy (fluid retention)
  • Obesity
  • Diabetes mellitus, hypothyroidism, rheumatoid arthritis
  • Trauma or prior wrist fracture

Clinical Features
  • Sensory: Numbness, tingling, pins-and-needles in the thumb, index, middle, and radial half of ring finger. Symptoms often worse at night or with wrist flexion.
  • Motor: Weakness of thumb abduction/opposition; in chronic cases, thenar muscle wasting.
Provocative signs:
  • Tinel’s sign: Percussion over median nerve produces tingling in fingers.
  • Phalen’s test: Wrist flexion reproduces symptoms.

Sensory distribution of median nerve. Click on image to enlarge

Diagnosis
Clinical diagnosis is usually sufficient. Nerve conduction studies (NCS)/electromyography (EMG) may be used if diagnosis is uncertain or surgery is considered. Imaging is rarely required.

Management
Conservative measures for mild to moderate CTS include:
  • Wrist splinting (futuro) especially at night
  • Activity modification
  • Analgesia / NSAIDs
  • Corticosteroid injection into carpal tunnel
Surgical (definitive) treatment:
  • Carpal tunnel release: Division of the transverse carpal ligament (flexor retinaculum) is indicated for persistent or severe symptoms, muscle wasting, or failed conservative therapy.

Prognosis
  • Early conservative management often successful.
  • Untreated CTS may progress to permanent sensory loss and thenar atrophy.
  • Surgical release generally has a high success rate, especially if performed before significant axonal loss.
NICE CKS00
Carpal tunnel syndrome
